message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Windows Component Object Model (COM) for local code execution and other malicious activities. COM is an inter-process communication (IPC) mechanism in Windows that allows interaction between software objects, typically DLLs or EXEs. Remote execution is possible via Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM). Attackers can abuse various COM interfaces to execute arbitrary code, create scheduled tasks, perform fileless downloads, and achieve persistence and privilege escalation. Notable malware such as Bumblebee, FunnyDream, Gamaredon Group, Gelsemium, HermeticWizard, InvisiMole, Milan, MuddyWater, Neoichor, POWERSTATS, Ramsay, TrickBot, and Ursnif have leveraged COM for these purposes. Mitigation strategies include restricting COM object access, monitoring and logging COM-related activities, employing application whitelisting, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious COM usage. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce the risk of exploitation.
